title: As AI Transforms Silicon Valley, Some Tech Workers Face Evaporating Financial Security - Slashdot
excerpt: The Washington Post describes a mid-tier executive at Meta as one of Silicon Valley's "winners" whose financial security suddenly "evaporated" as their workforce "pushed headlong into AI and heavy job cuts," creating a transformed job market. "Her ex-husband, a designer at Meta who was laid off in ...
content_html:
The Washington Post describes a mid-tier executive at Meta as one of Silicon Valley's "winners" whose financial security suddenly "evaporated" as their workforce "pushed headlong into AI and heavy job cuts," creating a transformed job market. "Her ex-husband, a designer at Meta who was laid off in 2020, eventually gave up looking for jobs in his profession. He now lifts boxes at a warehouse." Layoffs.fyi, which tracks announced job cuts, counts more than 800,000 tech workers laid off since 2022, including large staff reductions in recent months at Meta, Microsoft, Oracle and Amazon... "There's this whole tranche of people who've been quite used to being among the most upwardly mobile in society who are all of a sudden saying, 'Now I'm the guy on the streetâs'" said Oliver Raskin, who founded Silicon Valley market research consultancy Signalcraft Insights and has surveyed attitudes in the tech labor force... "The rise of AI, especially, is bound to change the workplace radically," [said Georgetown University historian Joseph McCartin]. "But the way it's going to happen is similar to how technology transformed the auto industry." Ruth Milkman, a labor sociologist at the City University of New York, said that technology workers are getting a dose of what workers in other industries have long complained about: jobs that feel unsteady or rob them of autonomy. "Low-wage workers are used to it," she said...

Many layoffs at technology companies are probably a hangover effect from over-hiring in prior years, experts say. And they don't account for a spotty recent increase in hiring in the information industry, which includes employment of software developers and jobs in media and entertainment. Digging deeper, though, some economists say there are signs that Silicon Valley and other technology-reliant parts of the American economy have reached a turning point where they are growing without needing as many people. The notion was encapsulated in a recent talk that ricocheted through group chats across the tech industry: In it, a partner at the start-up incubator Y Combinator heralded a new generation of AI-first companies that will only need human labor for "novel situations," "ethical considerations" and "high-stakes moments."

Gad Levanon, chief economist at the labor research nonprofit Burning Glass Institute, said that the number of hours worked in the information sector has dipped since 2022, while the sector's economic output has increased by about 8 percent a year — more than three times the overall growth rate of the U.S. economy. He says the data reveals a sea change in industries, including technology and finance, toward doing more work with the same or fewer people — one that is spreading to other professional classes. "That's the new reality for white-collar and tech-exposed work: output up, headcount flat or down," Levanon said...

Raskin, who has worked in the tech world since the late '90s, said that even though the current moment feels unsettling to many, he's hopeful that it's an early chapter in an evolving story. "It's happened many times before," he said, "that something implodes and all these people lose jobs, but then that talent gets cycled into whatever the next thing is — into a new wave of prosperity."


In the article tech entrepreneur Anil Dash quips that Silicon Valley techies are "are guinea pigs for what tech dudes want to do to everyone."

"Her ex-husband, a designer at Meta who was laid off in 2020, eventually gave up looking for jobs in his profession. He now lifts boxes at a warehouse." Layoffs.fyi, which tracks announced job cuts, counts more than 800,000 tech workers laid off since 2022, including large staff reductions in recent months at Meta, Microsoft, Oracle and Amazon... "There's this whole tranche of people who've been quite used to being among the most upwardly mobile in society who are all of a sudden saying, 'Now I'm the guy on the streetâs'" said Oliver Raskin, who founded Silicon Valley market research consultancy Signalcraft Insights and has surveyed attitudes in the tech labor force... "The rise of AI, especially, is bound to change the workplace radically," [said Georgetown University historian Joseph McCartin]. "But the way it's going to happen is similar to how technology transformed the auto industry." Ruth Milkman, a labor sociologist at the City University of New York, said that technology workers are getting a dose of what workers in other industries have long complained about: jobs that feel unsteady or rob them of autonomy. "Low-wage workers are used to it," she said... Many layoffs at technology companies are probably a hangover effect from over-hiring in prior years, experts say. And they don't account for a spotty recent increase in hiring in the information industry, which includes employment of software developers and jobs in media and entertainment. Digging deeper, though, some economists say there are signs that Silicon Valley and other technology-reliant parts of the American economy have reached a turning point where they are growing without needing as many people. The notion was encapsulated in a recent talk that ricocheted through group chats across the tech industry: In it, a partner at the start-up incubator Y Combinator heralded a new generation of AI-first companies that will only need human labor for "novel situations," "ethical considerations" and "high-stakes moments." Gad Levanon, chief economist at the labor research nonprofit Burning Glass Institute, said that the number of hours worked in the information sector has dipped since 2022, while the sector's economic output has increased by about 8 percent a year — more than three times the overall growth rate of the U.S. economy. He says the data reveals a sea change in industries, including technology and finance, toward doing more work with the same or fewer people — one that is spreading to other professional classes. "That's the new reality for white-collar and tech-exposed work: output up, headcount flat or down," Levanon said... Raskin, who has worked in the tech world since the late '90s, said that even though the current moment feels unsettling to many, he's hopeful that it's an early chapter in an evolving story. "It's happened many times before," he said, "that something implodes and all these people lose jobs, but then that talent gets cycled into whatever the next thing is — into a new wave of prosperity." In the article tech entrepreneur Anil Dash quips that Silicon Valley techies are "are guinea pigs for what tech dudes want to do to everyone."

sentences.en: The Washington Post describes a mid-tier executive at Meta as one of Silicon Valley's "winners" whose financial security suddenly "evaporated" as their workforce "pushed headlong into AI and heavy job cuts," creating a transformed job market.
sentences.zh: 《华盛顿邮报》将 Meta 的一位中层高管描述为硅谷的“赢家”之一，随着员工“一头扎进人工智能领域并大幅裁员”，他的财务安全突然“蒸发”，创造了一个转型的就业市场。

sentences.en: "Her ex-husband, a designer at Meta who was laid off in 2020, eventually gave up looking for jobs in his profession.
sentences.zh: “她的前夫是 Meta 的一名设计师，于 2020 年被解雇，最终放弃了寻找自己专业的工作。

sentences.en: He now lifts boxes at a warehouse."
sentences.zh: 他现在在仓库搬运箱子。”

sentences.en: Layoffs.fyi, which tracks announced job cuts, counts more than 800,000 tech workers laid off since 2022, including large staff reductions in recent months at Meta, Microsoft, Oracle and Amazon...
sentences.zh: 追踪已宣布裁员的 Layoffs.fyi 统计，自 2022 年以来，已有超过 80 万名科技工人被解雇，其中包括近几个月来 Meta、微软、甲骨文和亚马逊的大规模裁员……

sentences.en: "There's this whole tranche of people who've been quite used to being among the most upwardly mobile in society who are all of a sudden saying, 'Now I'm the guy on the streetâs'" said Oliver Raskin, who founded Silicon Valley market research consultancy Signalcraft Insights and has surveyed attitudes in the tech labor force...
sentences.zh: 奥利弗·拉斯金 (Oliver Raskin) 创立了硅谷市场研究咨询公司 Signalcraft Insights，并对科技劳动力的态度进行了调查。

sentences.en: "The rise of AI, especially, is bound to change the workplace radically," [said Georgetown University historian Joseph McCartin].
sentences.zh: “尤其是人工智能的兴起，势必会从根本上改变工作场所，”[乔治城大学历史学家约瑟夫·麦卡汀说]。

sentences.en: "But the way it's going to happen is similar to how technology transformed the auto industry."
sentences.zh: “但它发生的方式类似于技术如何改变汽车行业。”

sentences.en: Ruth Milkman, a labor sociologist at the City University of New York, said that technology workers are getting a dose of what workers in other industries have long complained about: jobs that feel unsteady or rob them of autonomy.
sentences.zh: 纽约城市大学的劳工社会学家露丝·米尔克曼 (Ruth Milkman) 表示，技术工人正面临着其他行业工人长期以来抱怨的情况：工作不稳定或剥夺了他们的自主权。

sentences.en: "Low-wage workers are used to it," she said...
sentences.zh: “低薪工人已经习惯了，”她说……

sentences.en: Many layoffs at technology companies are probably a hangover effect from over-hiring in prior years, experts say.
sentences.zh: 专家表示，科技公司的许多裁员可能是前几年过度招聘的后遗症。

sentences.en: And they don't account for a spotty recent increase in hiring in the information industry, which includes employment of software developers and jobs in media and entertainment.
sentences.zh: 而且它们并没有考虑到信息行业近期招聘人数的不稳定增长，其中包括软件开发人员的就业以及媒体和娱乐行业的就业机会。

sentences.en: Digging deeper, though, some economists say there are signs that Silicon Valley and other technology-reliant parts of the American economy have reached a turning point where they are growing without needing as many people.
sentences.zh: 不过，深入挖掘后，一些经济学家表示，有迹象表明，硅谷和美国经济中其他依赖技术的部分已经达到了一个转折点，它们在不需要那么多人的情况下实现了增长。

sentences.en: The notion was encapsulated in a recent talk that ricocheted through group chats across the tech industry: In it, a partner at the start-up incubator Y Combinator heralded a new generation of AI-first companies that will only need human labor for "novel situations," "ethical considerations" and "high-stakes moments."
sentences.zh: 这个概念在最近的一次演讲中得到了体现，该演讲在整个科技行业的群聊中引起了广泛反响：在演讲中，初创企业孵化器 Y Combinator 的一位合作伙伴预示了新一代人工智能优先公司的出现，这些公司只需要人力来应对“新情况”、“道德考虑”和“高风险时刻”。

sentences.en: Gad Levanon, chief economist at the labor research nonprofit Burning Glass Institute, said that the number of hours worked in the information sector has dipped since 2022, while the sector's economic output has increased by about 8 percent a year — more than three times the overall growth rate of the U.S. economy.
sentences.zh: 非营利性劳工研究组织 Burning Glass Institute 首席经济学家加德·莱瓦农 (Gad Levanon) 表示，自 2022 年以来，信息行业的工作时间有所下降，而该行业的经济产出每年增长约 8%，是美国经济总体增长率的三倍多。

sentences.en: He says the data reveals a sea change in industries, including technology and finance, toward doing more work with the same or fewer people — one that is spreading to other professional classes.
sentences.zh: 他说，这些数据揭示了包括技术和金融在内的行业发生的巨大变化，即用相同或更少的人完成更多的工作——这种变化正在蔓延到其他专业阶层。

sentences.en: "That's the new reality for white-collar and tech-exposed work: output up, headcount flat or down," Levanon said...
sentences.zh: 莱瓦农说：“这就是白领和科技工作的新现实：产出增加，员工人数持平或减少。”

sentences.en: Raskin, who has worked in the tech world since the late '90s, said that even though the current moment feels unsettling to many, he's hopeful that it's an early chapter in an evolving story.
sentences.zh: 自 90 年代末以来一直在科技界工作的拉斯金表示，尽管当前的时刻让许多人感到不安，但他希望这是一个不断发展的故事的早期章节。

sentences.en: "It's happened many times before," he said, "that something implodes and all these people lose jobs, but then that talent gets cycled into whatever the next thing is — into a new wave of prosperity."
sentences.zh: “这种情况以前发生过很多次，”他说，“一些事情发生内爆，所有这些人都失去了工作，但随后人才又被循环进入下一波新的繁荣浪潮。”

sentences.en: In the article tech entrepreneur Anil Dash quips that Silicon Valley techies are "are guinea pigs for what tech dudes want to do to everyone."
sentences.zh: 科技企业家阿尼尔·达什 (Anil Dash) 在文章中打趣道，硅谷的技术人员“是技术人员想要为每个人做的事情的小白鼠”。

And they don't account for a spotty recent increase in hiring in the information industry, which includes employment of software developers and jobs in media and entertainment. Digging deeper, though, some economists say there are signs that Silicon Valley and other technology-reliant parts of the American economy have reached a turning point where they are growing without needing as many people. The notion was encapsulated in a recent talk that ricocheted through group chats across the tech industry: In it, a partner at the start-up incubator Y Combinator heralded a new generation of AI-first companies that will only need human labor for "novel situations," "ethical considerations" and "high-stakes moments."

He says the data reveals a sea change in industries, including technology and finance, toward doing more work with the same or fewer people — one that is spreading to other professional classes. "That's the new reality for white-collar and tech-exposed work: output up, headcount flat or down," Levanon said...

sentences.zh: 非营利性劳工研究组织 Burning Glass Institute 首席经济学家加德·莱瓦农 (Gad Levanon) 表示，自 2022 年以来，信息行业的工作时间有所下降，而该行业的经济产出每年增长约 8%，是美国经济总体增长率的三倍多。

sentences.zh: 他说，这些数据揭示了包括技术和金融在内的行业发生的巨大变化，即用相同或更少的人完成更多的工作——这种变化正在蔓延到其他专业阶层。

sentences.en: "That's the new reality for white-collar and tech-exposed work: output up, headcount flat or down," Levanon said...
sentences.zh: 莱瓦农说：“这就是白领和科技工作的新现实：产出增加，员工人数持平或减少。”

blocks.html_en: Raskin, who has worked in the tech world since the late '90s, said that even though the current moment feels unsettling to many, he's hopeful that it's an early chapter in an evolving story. "It's happened many times before," he said, "that something implodes and all these people lose jobs, but then that talent gets cycled into whatever the next thing is — into a new wave of prosperity."

sentences.en: Raskin, who has worked in the tech world since the late '90s, said that even though the current moment feels unsettling to many, he's hopeful that it's an early chapter in an evolving story.
sentences.zh: 自 90 年代末以来一直在科技界工作的拉斯金表示，尽管当前的时刻让许多人感到不安，但他希望这是一个不断发展的故事的早期章节。

sentences.en: "It's happened many times before," he said, "that something implodes and all these people lose jobs, but then that talent gets cycled into whatever the next thing is — into a new wave of prosperity."
sentences.zh: “这种情况以前发生过很多次，”他说，“一些事情发生内爆，所有这些人都失去了工作，但随后人才又被循环进入下一波新的繁荣浪潮。”

content_html_zh: 《华盛顿邮报》将 Meta 的一位中层高管描述为硅谷的“赢家”之一，随着员工“一头扎进人工智能领域并大幅裁员”，他的财务安全突然“蒸发”，创造了一个转型的就业市场。 “她的前夫是 Meta 的一名设计师，于 2020 年被解雇，最终放弃了寻找自己专业的工作。 他现在在仓库搬运箱子。” 追踪已宣布裁员的 Layoffs.fyi 统计，自 2022 年以来，已有超过 80 万名科技工人被解雇，其中包括近几个月来 Meta、微软、甲骨文和亚马逊的大规模裁员…… 奥利弗·拉斯金 (Oliver Raskin) 创立了硅谷市场研究咨询公司 Signalcraft Insights，并对科技劳动力的态度进行了调查。 “尤其是人工智能的兴起，势必会从根本上改变工作场所，”[乔治城大学历史学家约瑟夫·麦卡汀说]。 “但它发生的方式类似于技术如何改变汽车行业。” 纽约城市大学的劳工社会学家露丝·米尔克曼 (Ruth Milkman) 表示，技术工人正面临着其他行业工人长期以来抱怨的情况：工作不稳定或剥夺了他们的自主权。 “低薪工人已经习惯了，”她说…… 专家表示，科技公司的许多裁员可能是前几年过度招聘的后遗症。 而且它们并没有考虑到信息行业近期招聘人数的不稳定增长，其中包括软件开发人员的就业以及媒体和娱乐行业的就业机会。 不过，深入挖掘后，一些经济学家表示，有迹象表明，硅谷和美国经济中其他依赖技术的部分已经达到了一个转折点，它们在不需要那么多人的情况下实现了增长。 这个概念在最近的一次演讲中得到了体现，该演讲在整个科技行业的群聊中引起了广泛反响：在演讲中，初创企业孵化器 Y Combinator 的一位合作伙伴预示了新一代人工智能优先公司的出现，这些公司只需要人力来应对“新情况”、“道德考虑”和“高风险时刻”。 非营利性劳工研究组织 Burning Glass Institute 首席经济学家加德·莱瓦农 (Gad Levanon) 表示，自 2022 年以来，信息行业的工作时间有所下降，而该行业的经济产出每年增长约 8%，是美国经济总体增长率的三倍多。 他说，这些数据揭示了包括技术和金融在内的行业发生的巨大变化，即用相同或更少的人完成更多的工作——这种变化正在蔓延到其他专业阶层。 莱瓦农说：“这就是白领和科技工作的新现实：产出增加，员工人数持平或减少。” 自 90 年代末以来一直在科技界工作的拉斯金表示，尽管当前的时刻让许多人感到不安，但他希望这是一个不断发展的故事的早期章节。 “这种情况以前发生过很多次，”他说，“一些事情发生内爆，所有这些人都失去了工作，但随后人才又被循环进入下一波新的繁荣浪潮。” 科技企业家阿尼尔·达什 (Anil Dash) 在文章中打趣道，硅谷的技术人员“是技术人员想要为每个人做的事情的小白鼠”。
